headline: Washington survives Miami's aerial assault in defensive slugfest
body: Graham Mertz put on a surgical passing clinic for Miami, completing 30 of 53 attempts for 389 yards and three touchdowns despite operating behind an offense that managed just 35 rushing yards. The quarterback's accuracy and poise kept the Hurricanes in this game deep into the fourth quarter, with receiver Campen securing eight catches for 89 yards to give the passing game consistent targets. Yet for all that aerial firepower, Miami's inability to establish any ground game left them vulnerable to a Washington defense that bent but ultimately held when it mattered most.

Washington's answer was the opposite philosophy: methodical, ground-oriented football that refused to beat itself. Quarterback Brown needed only 17 pass attempts, completing 13 of them for 138 yards, while running back Washington carried 11 times for 62 yards in a game plan designed to control clock and field position. The Huskies totaled just 330 yards of offense but won because they took care of the football and forced Miami into the turnover column—the Hurricanes coughed it up once, a decisive margin in such a tight contest.

Defensively, Washington's Clemons anchored the unit with 12 tackles and a sack, setting the tone in the trenches where this game was truly decided. Miami could not manufacture the ground attack needed to relieve pressure on Mertz, and when you're forcing a quarterback to beat you through the air alone, even 389 yards and three touchdowns sometimes falls short. This was a game about complementary football, not individual brilliance, and Washington's staff executed a patient formula that suffocated Miami's explosive potential.
turningPoint: Miami's forced fumble in the fourth quarter represented the only turnover of the day and proved decisive in a game where field position and momentum mattered more than raw yardage.

unsungHero.name: Clemons
unsungHero.why: While Brown's efficiency earned him the starting role, Clemons' 12 tackles and disruptive presence in the middle imposed the physical will that allowed Washington's defensive scheme to suffocate Miami's passing attack and prevent the Hurricanes from establishing rhythm.
